original_abstract:
We introduce a new characterization of the NP complexity class called Quadratic Span Programs (QSPs), a natural extension of the span programs defined by Karchmer and Wigderson. Our primary goal is to facilitate the construction of succinct, easily verifiable non-interactive arguments for NP statements. To achieve this, QSPs utilize a novel approach to the arithmetization of Boolean circuits that yields dramatic performance improvements over traditional techniques.

Using QSPs, we construct a Non-Interactive Zero-Knowledge (NIZK) argument in the Common Reference String (CRS) model for Circuit-SAT. Our proof consists of just 7 group elements, while the CRS size and prover computation remain quasi-linear. These properties make the scheme highly practical, a conclusion supported by our implementation; specifically, our NIZK argument achieves the shortest proofs, the most efficient prover, and the fastest verifier of any currently known technique.

We further present a variant of our construction called Quadratic Arithmetic Programs (QAPs), which are designed to compute arithmetic circuits over large fields naturally. Finally, we demonstrate how QSPs and QAPs can be applied to the public verification of outsourced computation. In this scenario, a client can verify the results of a function computed by a server in significantly less time than it would take to perform the computation from scratch. The resulting schemes represent the most efficient general-purpose protocols for publicly verifiable computation to date.
humanized_abstract:
We introduce a new complexity class called Quadratic Span Programs (QSPs) that are a quadratic analogue of the span programs of Karchmer and Wigderson. We use these objects to attempt to build succinct, relatively easy to verify non-interactive arguments for NP statements. Our formulation of these objects allows for a surprisingly efficient arithmetization of Boolean circuits.

Using QSPs we give a Non-Interactive Zero-Knowledge (NIZK) argument for Circuit-SAT in the Common Reference String (CRS) model. The proof consists of only 7 group elements and CRS prover time is quasi-linear. Our protocol is in fact highly practical: it produces the shortest proofs; the fastest proof verification in the prover and also the fastest verifier of all known NIZK and ZK implementations.

This paper is the final part of a trilogy of papers where we gave new protocols for Verifiable Computation and for Homomorphic Encryption. Here we give some additional results including a variant of our protocols called Quadratic Arithmetic Programs (QAPs) which can be used to compute arithmetic circuits over large fields in a natural way. We also show how to use our QSP and QAP protocols for the public verification of outsourced computation. In this application a client wishes to check the output computed by a server for some reasonably specified function. Our protocols allow the client to compute the output bit of the function verified at the server immediately, in time much less than the time needed to compute the actual function output by the server. We obtain the most efficient general-purpose public verifiable computation protocol to date.

document.input:
We introduce a new characterization of the NP complexity class called Quadratic Span Programs (QSPs), a natural extension of the span programs defined by Karchmer and Wigderson. In this scenario, a client can verify the results of a function computed by a server in significantly less time than it would take to perform the computation from scratch.
